findings.recommendation: Higher entropy transactions are harder to trace. When possible, spend exact amounts to avoid change. Consider using CoinJoin to maximize ambiguity - but note that some exchanges may flag CoinJoin deposits.

findings.title: 1 wallet fingerprinting signal detected
findings.description: Transaction metadata reveals wallet characteristics: nSequence=0xffffffff (legacy, no locktime/RBF). Wallet identification helps chain analysts narrow down the software used, which combined with other data can aid in deanonymization.
findings.recommendation: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in. Wallets with millions of users (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum) create large anonymity sets where your transaction looks like millions of others. Niche wallets create small sets that narrow identification. The fingerprint itself is unavoidable; what matters is how many people share it.

findings.title: Legacy transaction version (nVersion=1)
findings.description: This transaction uses nVersion=1, which is uncommon in modern wallets. This narrows identification to legacy software or Wasabi Wallet.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that creates nVersion=2 transactions (BIP68-compliant). Most modern wallets (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, Ashigaru) use nVersion=2.

findings.title: No anti-fee-sniping protection (nLockTime=0)
findings.description: This transaction has nLockTime=0, meaning it lacks anti-fee-sniping protection. Most modern wallets set nLockTime to the current block height. Not using it fingerprints the wallet and slightly weakens network security.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.title: No anonymity set (all outputs unique)
findings.description: All 2 outputs have unique values. Each output is trivially distinguishable, which is typical for standard transactions.
findings.recommendation: This is normal for most transactions. CoinJoin creates larger anonymity sets for improved privacy.

findings.title: Uniform script types
findings.description: All inputs and outputs use the same script type, making change detection harder. An observer cannot use script type mismatch to identify the change output.
findings.recommendation: Continue using wallets that maintain consistent address types.

findings.title: Single input address
findings.description: This transaction uses a single input address, so the common-input-ownership heuristic does not apply. No address clustering is possible from inputs alone.
findings.recommendation: Keep using single-input transactions when possible.

recommendation.headline: Your wallet sends change back to the input address
recommendation.detail: Switch to a wallet that generates fresh change addresses for every transaction. This is a critical privacy failure - the change output is 100% identifiable.

findings.title: Same address in input and output - change revealed (1 of 27 outputs)
findings.description: 1 of 27 spendable outputs go back to an address that was also an input. This is a 100% deterministic link - the output to this address is certainly change, revealing which other outputs are payments and the exact payment amount.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that generates a new change address for every transaction (HD wallets). Never send change back to the same address. Sparrow, Wasabi, and Bitcoin Core all handle this correctly.

findings.title: Deterministic privacy failure - score capped
findings.description: A 100% certain privacy leak was detected. The score is capped at F because no amount of positive signals can offset a deterministic identification.
findings.recommendation: Fix the deterministic issue before addressing other findings.

findings.title: Very low transaction entropy

findings.recommendation: Higher entropy transactions are harder to trace. When possible, spend exact amounts to avoid change. Consider using CoinJoin to maximize ambiguity - but note that some exchanges may flag CoinJoin deposits.

findings.title: 3 different script types in transaction
findings.description: This transaction uses 3 different script types (v0_p2wpkh, p2pkh, p2sh). Mixing script types makes change detection easier and can fingerprint the wallet software.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that keeps the same address format for all outputs. Consistent formats eliminate this signal.

findings.title: Possible exchange batch withdrawal (27 outputs)
findings.description: This transaction has 1 input and 27 outputs, a pattern consistent with a centralized exchange batch withdrawal. Structural indicators: 3 different output script types; 27 unique output addresses; wide value spread across outputs. Exchange-origin UTXOs carry KYC linkage risk - the exchange knows your identity and can link your withdrawal to your account.
findings.recommendation: If these funds came from a KYC exchange, they are linked to your identity. Before spending, pass them through CoinJoin to break the chain analysis link. Never mix exchange-origin UTXOs with non-KYC UTXOs in the same transaction.

findings.title: Batch payment pattern: 1 input to 27 outputs
findings.description: This transaction sends from 1 input to 27 outputs. This pattern is common in exchange or service batch withdrawals. It reveals that the sender is making multiple payments simultaneously, which is typical of institutional or automated spending.
findings.recommendation: Batch payments are an efficiency optimization but reveal payment patterns. For privacy, consider making individual transactions or using PayJoin.

findings.title: Fee appears calculated using non-SegWit weight
findings.description: This SegWit transaction has a fee rate that aligns to a round number when calculated against raw byte size (1 sat/byte) but not when calculated correctly against virtual size (1.1 sat/vB). This suggests the wallet may not account for the SegWit discount, fingerprinting it as older software.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that properly estimates fees using virtual bytes (vB). Most modern wallets handle this correctly.

findings.title: RBF (Replace-by-Fee) signaled
findings.description: This transaction signals RBF replaceability (nSequence < 0xfffffffe). RBF is now standard across virtually all modern wallet software and is no longer a meaningful fingerprinting signal.
findings.recommendation: RBF is standard practice and reveals little about the sender. Note: if fee-bumped via RBF, comparing original and replacement transactions can reveal which output decreased (the change). For maximum privacy, set an adequate fee upfront to avoid the need for fee bumping.

findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.description: This transaction is part of a peel chain - a sequence of 2+ transactions each with 1 input and 2 outputs, where one output feeds the next transaction as its sole input. findings.recommendation: Use coin control to avoid combining UTXOs from different addresses. If consolidation is necessary, use CoinJoin first to break the link between source addresses.

findings.title: Consolidation: 10 inputs to 1 output
findings.description: This transaction consolidates 10 separate UTXOs into a single output. All input addresses are now permanently linked on-chain. An observer can see the entity's full UTXO set and total balance at this point in time.
findings.recommendation: If consolidation is necessary, run UTXOs through CoinJoin first to break address linkage. Consider splitting consolidation across multiple smaller transactions over time.

findings.title: RBF (Replace-by-Fee) signaled
findings.description: This transaction signals RBF replaceability (nSequence < 0xfffffffe). RBF is now standard across virtually all modern wallet software and is no longer a meaningful fingerprinting signal.
findings.recommendation: RBF is standard practice and reveals little about the sender. Note: if fee-bumped via RBF, comparing original and replacement transactions can reveal which output decreased (the change). For maximum privacy, set an adequate fee upfront to avoid the need for fee bumping.

findings.title: Legacy transaction version (nVersion=1)
findings.description: This transaction uses nVersion=1, which is uncommon in modern wallets. This narrows identification to legacy software or Wasabi Wallet.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that creates nVersion=2 transactions (BIP68-compliant). Most modern wallets (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, Ashigaru) use nVersion=2.

findings.title: No anti-fee-sniping protection (nLockTime=0)
findings.description: This transaction has nLockTime=0, meaning it lacks anti-fee-sniping protection. Most modern wallets set nLockTime to the current block height. Not using it fingerprints the wallet and slightly weakens network security.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.title: Changeless transaction (possible Branch-and-Bound selection)
findings.description: This transaction has multiple inputs but no change output. This is consistent with Branch-and-Bound (BnB) coin selection, which finds input combinations that exactly match the payment amount plus fee. BnB is used by Bitcoin Core and Sparrow. Changeless transactions are better for privacy since there is no change output to link back to the sender.
findings.recommendation: Changeless transactions are good for privacy. Continue using wallets with BnB coin selection when possible.

recommendation.headline: Your wallet sends change back to the input address
recommendation.detail: Switch to a wallet that generates fresh change addresses for every transaction. This is a critical privacy failure - the change output is 100% identifiable.

findings.title: Same address in input and output - change revealed (1 of 11 outputs)
findings.description: 1 of 11 spendable outputs go back to an address that was also an input. This is a 100% deterministic link - the output to this address is certainly change, revealing which other outputs are payments and the exact payment amount.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that generates a new change address for every transaction (HD wallets). Never send change back to the same address. Sparrow, Wasabi, and Bitcoin Core all handle this correctly.

findings.title: Deterministic privacy failure - score capped
findings.description: A 100% certain privacy leak was detected. The score is capped at F because no amount of positive signals can offset a deterministic identification.
findings.recommendation: Fix the deterministic issue before addressing other findings.

findings.title: Very low transaction entropy
findings.description: This transaction has near-zero entropy (0 bits, via multi-tier permutation estimate). There is essentially only one valid interpretation of the fund flow, making it trivial to trace.
findings.recommendation: Higher entropy transactions are harder to trace. When possible, spend exact amounts to avoid change. Consider using CoinJoin to maximize ambiguity - but note that some exchanges may flag CoinJoin deposits.

findings.title: 4 different script types in transaction
findings.description: This transaction uses 4 different script types (v0_p2wpkh, v0_p2wsh, p2sh, p2pkh). Mixing script types makes change detection easier and can fingerprint the wallet software.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that keeps the same address format for all outputs. Consistent formats eliminate this signal.

findings.title: Possible exchange batch withdrawal (11 outputs)
findings.description: This transaction has 1 input and 11 outputs, a pattern consistent with a centralized exchange batch withdrawal. Structural indicators: 4 different output script types; 11 unique output addresses; wide value spread across outputs. Exchange-origin UTXOs carry KYC linkage risk - the exchange knows your identity and can link your withdrawal to your account.
findings.recommendation: If these funds came from a KYC exchange, they are linked to your identity. Before spending, pass them through CoinJoin to break the chain analysis link. Never mix exchange-origin UTXOs with non-KYC UTXOs in the same transaction.

findings.title: 1 round amount output detected
findings.description: 1 of 11 outputs are round numbers. Round payment amounts make it trivial to distinguish payments from change, revealing the exact amount sent and which output is change.
findings.recommendation: Avoid sending round BTC amounts. Many wallets let you send exact sat amounts. Even adding a few random sats helps obscure the payment amount.

findings.title: 1 wallet fingerprinting signal detected
findings.description: Transaction metadata reveals wallet characteristics: nSequence=0xffffffff (legacy, no locktime/RBF). Wallet identification helps chain analysts narrow down the software used, which combined with other data can aid in deanonymization.
findings.recommendation: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in. Wallets with millions of users (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum) create large anonymity sets where your transaction looks like millions of others. Niche wallets create small sets that narrow identification. The fingerprint itself is unavoidable; what matters is how many people share it.

findings.title: Batch payment pattern: 1 input to 11 outputs
findings.description: This transaction sends from 1 input to 11 outputs. This pattern is common in exchange or service batch withdrawals. It reveals that the sender is making multiple payments simultaneously, which is typical of institutional or automated spending.
findings.recommendation: Batch payments are an efficiency optimization but reveal payment patterns. For privacy, consider making individual transactions or using PayJoin.

findings.title: Fee appears calculated using non-SegWit weight
findings.description: This SegWit transaction has a fee rate that aligns to a round number when calculated against raw byte size (1 sat/byte) but not when calculated correctly against virtual size (1.2 sat/vB). This suggests the wallet may not account for the SegWit discount, fingerprinting it as older software.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that properly estimates fees using virtual bytes (vB). Most modern wallets handle this correctly.

findings.title: Anonymity set: 2 equal outputs
findings.description: 2 outputs share the value 53,376 sats. This provides limited ambiguity. Equal-value groups: 2x 53,376 sats.
findings.recommendation: For stronger privacy, use CoinJoin to create larger anonymity sets (5+ equal outputs).

findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.title: Exchange batch withdrawal pattern detected
findings.description: This transaction has few inputs and many outputs with mixed address types, consistent with an exchange batch withdrawal. The sending entity is likely a centralized exchange processing multiple customer withdrawals.
findings.recommendation: If you received funds in this transaction, be aware the sending exchange can identify which output is your withdrawal. Consider mixing received funds before further on-chain activity.

findings.title: Single input address
findings.description: This transaction uses a single input address, so the common-input-ownership heuristic does not apply. No address clustering is possible from inputs alone.
findings.recommendation: Keep using single-input transactions when possible.

recommendation.headline: Break the payment chain - manage change individually
recommendation.detail: Making payments with change from previous payments links transactions in cascade, leaving a clear trail of your economic activity. Freeze the change and use a different UTXO for each payment. If you need to spend change, use it in collaborative transactions (PayJoin as a receiver, Stonewall) to increase ambiguity.

findings.title: Change output likely identifiable (medium confidence)
findings.description: 4 sub-heuristics point to a likely change output: change matches input address type; large value disparity between outputs; optimal change: output receives >90% of input value; shadow change: output much smaller than smallest input. Multiple signals agree, making change identification reliable. When the change output is known, the exact payment amount and recipient are revealed. RBF is signaled on this transaction. If fee-bumped via RBF, the change output value will decrease, confirming which output is change.
findings.recommendation: Use wallets with change output randomization. Avoid round payment amounts. Use the same address type for all outputs to eliminate the address-type-mismatch signal.

findings.title: Peel chain detected (2+ consecutive hops)
findings.description: This transaction is part of a peel chain - a sequence of 2+ transactions each with 1 input and 2 outputs, where one output feeds the next transaction as its sole input. The smaller output at each hop is typically the payment, making the entire payment history trivially traceable by following the chain.
findings.recommendation: Break the linear chain by using CoinJoin between payments. Batch multiple payments into a single transaction, or use PayJoin to add inputs from the recipient and destroy the peel pattern.

findings.title: Likely HodlHodl escrow release (2-of-3 multisig, fee pattern)
findings.description: This transaction matches the HodlHodl P2P exchange pattern: a 2-of-3 multisig input releasing to exactly 2 outputs, where the smaller output (0.81% of input) is consistent with HodlHodl's combined buyer+seller platform fee (typically 0.9-1.0%). The 2-of-3 multisig structure indicates buyer, seller, and HodlHodl arbitrator shared custody.
findings.recommendation: HodlHodl escrow transactions are identifiable on-chain due to the 2-of-3 multisig structure and characteristic fee pattern. For more private P2P trading, consider protocols that use Taproot-based escrow or Lightning-based settlement (e.g., RoboSats).

findings.recommendation: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in. Wallets with millions of users (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum) create large anonymity sets where your transaction looks like millions of others. Niche wallets create small sets that narrow identification. The fingerprint itself is unavoidable; what matters is how many people share it.

findings.title: No anti-fee-sniping protection (nLockTime=0)
findings.description: This transaction has nLockTime=0, meaning it lacks anti-fee-sniping protection. Most modern wallets set nLockTime to the current block height. Not using it fingerprints the wallet and slightly weakens network security.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.title: Uniform script types
findings.description: All inputs and outputs use the same script type, making change detection harder. An observer cannot use script type mismatch to identify the change output.
findings.recommendation: Continue using wallets that maintain consistent address types.

findings.title: Single input address
findings.description: This transaction uses a single input address, so the common-input-ownership heuristic does not apply. No address clustering is possible from inputs alone.
findings.recommendation: Keep using single-input transactions when possible.

recommendation.headline: Avoid combining UTXOs from different sources
recommendation.detail: Every time you consolidate, you reveal additional information about your economic activity to everyone who sent you each coin joined in this transaction. Choose a single UTXO that covers the payment. If you must consolidate, do it with coins from the same origin.

findings.title: 10 input addresses clustered via CIOH
findings.description: This transaction combines inputs from 10 different addresses. Chain analysis firms will assume these 10 addresses belong to the same entity. This assumption is probabilistic but widely applied in commercial chain surveillance.
findings.recommendation: Use coin control to avoid combining UTXOs from different addresses. If consolidation is necessary, use CoinJoin first to break the link between source addresses.

findings.title: Consolidation: 16 inputs to 1 output
findings.description: This transaction consolidates 16 separate UTXOs into a single output. All input addresses are now permanently linked on-chain. An observer can see the entity's full UTXO set and total balance at this point in time.
findings.recommendation: If consolidation is necessary, run UTXOs through CoinJoin first to break address linkage. Consider splitting consolidation across multiple smaller transactions over time.

findings.title: Cross-type consolidation (3 script types)
findings.description: This consolidation combines UTXOs from 3 different script types (p2pkh, v0_p2wpkh, p2sh). This links addresses from different wallet generations or software together, giving chain observers additional clustering signals beyond basic CIOH.
findings.recommendation: Keep UTXOs from different address types separate. If you must consolidate across types, CoinJoin each type independently first.

findings.title: Fee appears calculated using non-SegWit weight
findings.description: This SegWit transaction has a fee rate that aligns to a round number when calculated against raw byte size (1 sat/byte) but not when calculated correctly against virtual size (1.2 sat/vB). This suggests the wallet may not account for the SegWit discount, fingerprinting it as older software.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that properly estimates fees using virtual bytes (vB). Most modern wallets handle this correctly.

findings.title: Mixed witness and non-witness inputs

findings.recommendation: Use a single address type for all inputs. Migrate legacy UTXOs to SegWit (bech32) before spending them alongside native SegWit inputs.

findings.title: Legacy transaction version (nVersion=1)

findings.title: Changeless transaction (possible Branch-and-Bound selection)

This is consistent with Branch-and-Bound (BnB) coin selection, which finds input combinations that exactly match the payment amount plus fee. BnB is used by Bitcoin Core and Sparrow. Changeless transactions are better for privacy since there is no change output to link back to the sender.

findings.title: JoinMarket CoinJoin: 666 equal outputs of 0.0000033 BTC
findings.description: This transaction has 2 inputs and 667 outputs with 666 outputs at the same value (0.0000033 BTC), consistent with a JoinMarket CoinJoin. Unlike Whirlpool (fixed pool sizes) or WabiSabi (multiple denomination tiers), JoinMarket uses a single arbitrary denomination chosen by the taker, with separate change outputs for each maker and the taker. The 1 change output is trivially linkable to specific inputs via subset-sum analysis, since the taker pays an on-chain fee to makers. An observer can match each change to its corresponding input by finding which input subsets produce the denomination plus change. The anonymity set is 666 (the number of equal outputs), which is lower than typical Whirlpool (5 per round) or WabiSabi (50+) CoinJoins.
findings.recommendation: JoinMarket provides privacy through its decentralized maker/taker model, but has known weaknesses: change outputs are vulnerable to subset-sum analysis (CoinJoin Sudoku), and the single denomination makes the transaction pattern identifiable. Never consolidate change outputs with mixed outputs - this undoes the mixing. For stronger privacy, consider multiple rounds of mixing or using Whirlpool/WabiSabi which avoid on-chain fee leakage. Centralized exchanges including Binance, Coinbase, Gemini, Bitstamp, Swan Bitcoin, Bitvavo, Bitfinex, and BitMEX have been documented flagging, freezing, or closing accounts for CoinJoin-associated deposits. This list is not exhaustive - other exchanges may have similar policies. Some exchanges retroactively flag CoinJoin activity months or years after the transaction. For safe off-ramping, consider decentralized alternatives like Bisq, RoboSats, or Hodl Hodl that do not apply chain surveillance.

findings.recommendation: Never consolidate post-mix UTXOs. Spend each CoinJoin output individually in separate transactions. Use coin control (available in Sparrow, Ashigaru) to select exactly one post-mix UTXO per payment.

findings.title: 18 unnecessary inputs used
findings.description: This transaction used 23 inputs, but only 5 were needed to cover the outputs and fee. The 18 extra inputs unnecessarily link additional addresses together via CIOH, suggesting poor coin selection or intentional consolidation.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet with coin control to select only the minimum UTXOs needed. Avoid auto-selection modes that sweep all available UTXOs into every transaction.

findings.title: Anomalous I/O ratio: 23 inputs to 2 outputs
findings.description: This transaction has 23 inputs but only 2 outputs (ratio 11.5:1). A normal payment uses 1-2 inputs. The high input count suggests consolidation is being combined with a payment, linking many addresses together unnecessarily.
findings.recommendation: Separate consolidation from payments. Consolidate UTXOs in a dedicated transaction (ideally after CoinJoin), then make payments from the consolidated output.

findings.title: 1 wallet fingerprinting signal detected
findings.description: Transaction metadata reveals wallet characteristics: nSequence=0xfffffffd (RBF enabled). Wallet identification helps chain analysts narrow down the software used, which combined with other data can aid in deanonymization.
findings.recommendation: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in. Wallets with millions of users (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum) create large anonymity sets where your transaction looks like millions of others. Niche wallets create small sets that narrow identification. The fingerprint itself is unavoidable; what matters is how many people share it.

findings.title: Transaction entropy: 1 bits
findings.description: This transaction has 1 bits of entropy (via multi-tier permutation estimate), meaning there are approximately ~2 possible interpretations of the fund flow. Higher entropy makes chain analysis less reliable. Entropy per UTXO: 0.04 bits (25 UTXOs).
findings.recommendation: When possible, spend exact amounts to avoid change outputs. For significantly higher entropy, consider CoinJoin - but note that some exchanges may flag CoinJoin deposits.

findings.title: RBF (Replace-by-Fee) signaled
findings.description: This transaction signals RBF replaceability (nSequence < 0xfffffffe). RBF is now standard across virtually all modern wallet software and is no longer a meaningful fingerprinting signal.
findings.recommendation: RBF is standard practice and reveals little about the sender. Note: if fee-bumped via RBF, comparing original and replacement transactions can reveal which output decreased (the change). For maximum privacy, set an adequate fee upfront to avoid the need for fee bumping.

findings.title: No anti-fee-sniping protection (nLockTime=0)
findings.description: This transaction has nLockTime=0, meaning it lacks anti-fee-sniping protection. Most modern wallets set nLockTime to the current block height. Not using it fingerprints the wallet and slightly weakens network security.
findings.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).

findings.title: No anonymity set (all outputs unique)
findings.description: All 2 outputs have unique values. Each output is trivially distinguishable, which is typical for standard transactions.
findings.recommendation: This is normal for most transactions. CoinJoin creates larger anonymity sets for improved privacy.

findings.title: Uniform script types
findings.description: All inputs and outputs use the same script type, making change detection harder. An observer cannot use script type mismatch to identify the change output.
findings.recommendation: Continue using wallets that maintain consistent address types.

findings.title: Single input address
findings.description: This transaction uses a single input address, so the common-input-ownership heuristic does not apply. No address clustering is possible from inputs alone.
findings.recommendation: Keep using single-input transactions when possible.
